children.name: Agelaius
children.description: The red-winged and yellow-winged blackbirds — medium-sized icterids named for their distinctive shoulder patches. Males are black with bold red or yellow epaulets, which they flash aggressively during territorial displays. They are highly polygynous, with dominant males defending territories that attract multiple females, who build nests woven into cattails and marsh vegetation.

children.name: Quiscalus
children.description: The grackles — large, glossy blackbirds with long, keeled tails and pale yellow eyes. They are among the most intelligent of all songbirds, capable of solving complex puzzles and remembering individual human faces. Grackles are highly social, forming enormous noisy roosts, and have adapted brilliantly to urban and suburban environments.

children.name: Molothrus
children.description: The cowbirds — obligate brood parasites that lay their eggs in the nests of other passerine species. They are named for their historical association with bison herds, following the great herds to feed on insects flushed by their movement. A single female may lay several dozen eggs in a season, each in a different host nest, never building a nest or rearing her own young.

children.name: Icterus
children.description: The New World orioles — brilliantly coloured blackbirds with a striking contrast of black and bright yellow, orange, or chestnut. They build remarkable hanging pouch nests woven from plant fibres, suspended from branch tips. Males sing rich, fluty whistles that carry through the treetops. They are primarily frugivorous and insectivorous, and many species undertake long migrations between breeding and wintering grounds.

children.name: Xanthocephalus
children.description: The yellow-headed blackbird — a striking monotypic genus named for the male's vivid yellow head and breast. It breeds in the marshes of western and central North America, often alongside Red-winged Blackbirds. Males defend territories in deep-water cattail marshes and perform impressive flight displays, singing a grating, strained song while hanging from cattail stems.

children.name: Dolichonyx
children.description: The bobolink — a monotypic genus of Grassland icterid with one of the longest migrations of any passerine. The male's breeding plumage is unique among North American birds: black below and on the head, with a buff nape and white back and rump. The species is named for its bubbling, ecstatic song, which pours forth in sustained, tumbling phrases from the male's flight display over hayfields and prairies.

children.name: Sturnella
children.description: The meadowlarks — stocky, ground-dwelling icterids with bright yellow underparts and a bold black V on the breast. Despite their name they are not larks but true blackbirds, and are among the most iconic birds of American grasslands. Their clear, fluting songs carry across open fields, and they are more often heard than seen, perching on fence posts and low trees to deliver their melodies.

children.name: Sturnella neglecta
children.description: Category:Symbols of Montana Category:Symbols of Wyoming.

children.name: Leistes
children.description: A genus of South American meadowlarks separated from Sturnella by genetic evidence, with bright red or yellow underparts and a strong preference for grasslands. Males are highly territorial and perform conspicuous flight displays.

children.name: Euphagus
children.description: The American blackbirds — a small genus of two species that bridge the gap between grackles and true blackbirds. They are medium-sized icterids with glossy black plumage, pale eyes, and relatively slender bills. One species is a common bird of towns and farms, while the other has suffered a dramatic and poorly understood population decline.

children.name: Cacicus
children.description: The caciques — medium-sized icterids with predominantly black plumage accented by bright yellow, red, or orange patches. They are social birds that build long, hanging pouch nests in colonies, often near wasp nests for protection from predators.

children.name: Psarocolius
children.description: The oropendolas — large icterids with striking combination of black, chestnut, and yellow plumage, and long, pointed bills with colourful tips. They are colonial breeders that weave extraordinary hanging nests up to two metres long, suspended from isolated trees.

children.name: Gymnomystax
children.description: The oriole blackbird — a monotypic genus of striking black-and-yellow icterid found in South America. Despite its name, it is not a true oriole but a blackbird with a similar colour pattern.

children.name: Dives
children.description: A genus of medium-sized, entirely black icterids with a strong, musical song and a preference for open habitats. They are the most thrush-like in appearance among the blackbirds.

children.name: Cercomochus
children.description: Cercomochus — a genus of new world blackbirds.

children.name: Cercomochus nebulosus
children.description: A small, elusive bird with a strikingly patterned plumage. Found in dense forests and wetlands, it has become difficult to study due to its cryptic nature.

children.name: Chrysotheretia
children.description: Chrysotheretia — a genus of new world blackbirds.

children.name: Chrysotheretia garrula
children.description: A vibrant bird with a distinctive yellow throat and black streaks on its breast. They are known for their melodious song, which can be heard throughout the summer.

children.name: Piranga luteola
children.description: A striking bird with a bright yellow throat and upperparts, this species is found in riparian areas and wetlands. Its cheerful song adds to the chorus of spring.

children.name: Halcyon fulva
children.description: This bird is known for its bright yellow throat and upperparts. It is a specialized feeder, often seen in riparian areas.

children.name: Daphoenus
children.description: Daphoenus — a genus of new world blackbirds.

children.name: Daphoenus obscurus
children.description: This tiny bird is a specialist feeder, spending much of its time probing into the leaf litter for insect prey. It has a unique call that can be heard throughout the forest understory.

children.name: Chrysolophus leucophrys
children.description: This unique bird is named for its distinctive song and feeding habits. It is a specialized feeder, often seen flying over wetlands in search of insects.

children.name: Ambrosiceps cassinii
children.description: A small, olive-green bird with a distinctive white eye ring and prominent yellow bill. Found in coastal scrub and wetlands from Nova Scotia to Texas.

children.name: Chlorospingus flavescens
children.description: A small, olive-green bird with a yellow wash on the underparts and distinctive white eye ring. Found in open woodlands from New Mexico to Florida.

children.name: Eurymachus
children.description: Eurymachus — a genus of new world blackbirds.

children.name: Eurymachus eximius
children.description: This warbler has a unique habitat preference, being found only in the cavities of hollow trees. Its striking plumage and song are characteristic features of this species.

children.name: Leptopumachus
children.description: Leptopumachus — a genus of new world blackbirds.

children.name: Leptopumachus lepidus
children.description: Known for its iridescent feathers and melodious song, this species is found in the southern regions of North America. Its bright plumage makes it easily recognizable.

children.name: Terekeca ciliata
children.description: This warbler is known for its crested head and striking plumage. It can often be found in the canopy of mature forests, where it feeds on insects.

children.name: Euphronia canorus
children.description: This bird is known for its distinctive call and overall patterned plumage. It is a specialized feeder, often seen in wetlands.

children.name: Melozone
children.description: Melozone — a genus of new world blackbirds.

children.name: Melozone maculatus
children.description: This waterthrush is named for its spotted plumage. It is a secretive bird, often only glimpsed by those who listen carefully to the sounds of running streams and water.

children.name: Polioptila
children.description: Polioptila — a genus of new world blackbirds.

children.name: Polioptila rufa
children.description: This small warbler is named for its striking black and white plumage. It is a secretive feeder, often seen in dense foliage. Its vocalizations are characteristic of the eastern forests.

children.name: Certhia
children.description: Certhia — a genus of new world blackbirds.

children.name: Certhia melanocephala
children.description: This bird is distinguished by its striking black cap and overall brown plumage. It is a specialized feeder, often seen in coniferous forests.

children.name: Drepanospiza albiventris
children.description: This species is known for its snowy-white plumage and distinctive song. It is a specialized feeder, often seen in dense coniferous forests.
